_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against ACG 2023 Rubio-Tapia guideline, ESsCD 2019, Singh et al Clin Gastro Hepatol 2018 meta-analysis (~1.4% seroprevalence, 0.7% biopsy-confirmed), Lebwohl Lancet 2018 seminar, and Catassi Gastroenterology 2024 review. HLA, serology, Marsh grading, and ESPGHAN no-biopsy thresholds checked against current guideline text.

h1Title: Celiac Disease: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Celiac disease is an immune-mediated enteropathy in which dietary gluten triggers villous atrophy of the small intestine in genetically predisposed people who carry HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8. Global seroprevalence sits near 1.4% and biopsy-confirmed prevalence near 0.7% (Singh et al, Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol 2018), yet roughly 70% of cases remain undiagnosed because presentations are often non-classical — iron deficiency, fatigue, osteopenia, infertility, or transaminitis rather than the textbook diarrhea and weight loss. Diagnosis rests on serology (IgA tissue transglutaminase) plus duodenal biopsy, performed while the patient is still eating gluten. Treatment is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet, which produces mucosal healing in roughly 65-85% of adults within two years and prevents long-term complications including osteoporosis, anemia, and enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma.

keyStats.prevalence: Global biopsy-confirmed ~0.7%, seroprevalence ~1.4% (Singh meta-analysis, Clin Gastroenterol Hepatol 2018)
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio roughly 2-3:1; first-degree relatives carry ~10% lifetime risk
keyStats.avgAge: Two peaks: early childhood (6 months-2 years after gluten introduction) and ages 30-50 in adults
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 80-90 million globally; roughly 70% remain undiagnosed

definition: Celiac disease (ICD-10: K90.0) is a chronic immune-mediated enteropathy of the small intestine triggered by ingestion of gluten — the storage protein found in wheat, barley, and rye — in genetically susceptible individuals carrying the HLA-DQ2 or HLA-DQ8 class II haplotypes (present in over 99% of patients). Deamidated gluten peptides are presented to CD4+ T cells, driving an inflammatory cascade that destroys absorptive villi, deepens crypts, and infiltrates the epithelium with lymphocytes. The histologic spectrum is graded by the Marsh-Oberhuber classification from Marsh 0 (normal) through Marsh III (partial, subtotal, or total villous atrophy), with Marsh II or higher required for diagnosis. The resulting malabsorption affects iron, folate, vitamin B12, calcium, vitamin D, and fat-soluble vitamins. Celiac disease is classified as a systemic autoimmune disorder, not merely a gut condition: dermatologic, neurologic, hematologic, hepatic, and reproductive manifestations are common. It overlaps mechanistically with other HLA-DQ2/DQ8-linked autoimmune diseases including type 1 diabetes and autoimmune thyroiditis. Management is led by gastroenterology with input from dietetics, endocrinology, and dermatology depending on extra-intestinal involvement.

typesClassification.description: Presents with signs and symptoms of malabsorption — chronic diarrhea, weight loss, steatorrhea, growth failure in children. Now represents under half of new adult diagnoses.

Predominant extra-intestinal features without diarrhea — iron deficiency anemia, osteopenia, transaminitis, infertility, neurologic symptoms, fatigue, or dermatitis herpetiformis. Accounts for over 50% of new adult diagnoses (Catassi 2024).
Positive serology and Marsh II-III histology in a person with no recognizable symptoms, usually identified by family screening or coincidental testing.
Positive celiac-specific serology and HLA-DQ2/DQ8 but normal or near-normal duodenal histology. Roughly one third progress to overt disease over 5-10 years.

Persistent or recurrent symptoms and villous atrophy despite a strict gluten-free diet for 12+ months, after excluding ongoing gluten exposure and alternative diagnoses. Affects 1-2% of adults with celiac disease and divides into Type I (polyclonal lymphocytes) and Type II (clonal aberrant intraepithelial lymphocytes, with high risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma).

primarySymptoms: Chronic or intermittent diarrhea, often pale, bulky, and foul-smelling due to fat malabsorption — present in around 40-50% of new adult diagnoses, less common than a generation ago.
Abdominal pain, bloating, and excessive flatulence after meals containing wheat, barley, or rye.
Unintentional weight loss in adults, or faltering growth and short stature in children.
Iron deficiency anemia unresponsive to oral iron — present in up to 40% of newly diagnosed adults and often the only sign.
Persistent fatigue and reduced exercise tolerance, frequently attributed to anemia or B-vitamin deficiency.
Dermatitis herpetiformis — intensely itchy, symmetric vesicular rash on elbows, knees, buttocks, and scalp; pathognomonic skin form of celiac disease, present in about 10-15% of patients.
Recurrent mouth ulcers (aphthous stomatitis) and enamel defects on permanent teeth — common in pediatric and young adult patients.
Bone pain, fragility fractures, or osteopenia/osteoporosis on DEXA, driven by calcium and vitamin D malabsorption.
Neurologic features including peripheral neuropathy, gluten ataxia, and persistent 'brain fog' or difficulty concentrating.
Reproductive problems — delayed menarche, infertility, recurrent miscarriage, and earlier menopause — reported at roughly 2-4x background rates in untreated celiac disease.
Unexplained elevation of liver transaminases (ALT/AST), found in 15-25% of newly diagnosed adults and usually resolves on a gluten-free diet.
Constipation rather than diarrhea in a meaningful minority, particularly in older adults and children with celiac disease.
earlyWarningSigns: Iron deficiency anemia in an adult that fails to correct on oral iron — strongest single trigger for celiac screening
Persistent abdominal bloating, gas, or loose stools that worsen with bread, pasta, or beer
Low DEXA bone density in a person under 50 without other risk factors
Unexplained transaminitis (mildly raised ALT/AST) with a normal abdominal ultrasound
A first-degree relative diagnosed with celiac disease — sibling, parent, or child
emergencySigns: Severe dehydration with electrolyte disturbance from acute high-volume diarrhea — celiac crisis is rare in adults but well-described in young children
Acute abdominal pain with fever or weight loss in established celiac disease — possible ulcerative jejunoileitis or enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL)
New-onset gastrointestinal bleeding or palpable abdominal mass on a strict gluten-free diet — urgent investigation for small bowel adenocarcinoma or lymphoma
Tetany, carpopedal spasm, or seizures from severe hypocalcemia or magnesium depletion at first presentation

causes.cause: Gluten exposure in genetically susceptible individuals
causes.description: The necessary environmental trigger. Gliadin and related prolamins in wheat, barley, and rye resist digestion and reach the lamina propria where, after deamidation by tissue transglutaminase, they bind HLA-DQ2 or DQ8 with high affinity and drive a T-cell response against intestinal epithelium.

Over 99% of patients carry HLA-DQ2 (about 90%) or HLA-DQ8 (about 9%). These class II MHC molecules are required to present deamidated gluten peptides to CD4+ T cells. About 30-40% of the general population carries these haplotypes, but only 1-3% of carriers develop celiac disease, indicating that other genes and environmental factors are required.

Genome-wide studies identify over 40 non-HLA loci associated with celiac disease, including genes regulating T-cell activation, cytokine signaling, and innate immunity. Cumulatively these explain another 10-15% of heritability beyond HLA.

Gluten exposure transiently increases intestinal permeability via the zonulin pathway, allowing gluten peptides to reach the lamina propria. Patients with celiac disease show heightened and persistent zonulin-driven permeability compared with controls.

Pediatric studies show that infant feeding patterns, early intestinal infections (especially rotavirus and reovirus), antibiotic exposure, and gut microbiome composition influence the risk of progression from susceptibility to overt disease, though no single environmental factor has been shown to fully prevent or trigger celiac disease.

Tissue transglutaminase 2 (tTG) deamidates gluten peptides, increasing their affinity for HLA-DQ2/DQ8. The same enzyme then becomes the target of the disease-specific IgA antibody response that defines serologic diagnosis.

riskFactors.description: Necessary but not sufficient — present in over 99% of celiac patients versus 30-40% of the general population. Absence essentially excludes celiac disease, which is why HLA testing has a high negative predictive value.

Sibling, parent, or child of a confirmed patient has approximately 10% lifetime risk (Singh 2015 meta-analysis). Second-degree relatives carry roughly 3-5% risk. ACG 2023 recommends screening symptomatic first-degree relatives.
Around 5-10% of people with type 1 diabetes have biopsy-confirmed celiac disease, reflecting shared HLA-DQ2/DQ8 background. ISPAD recommends periodic celiac screening in this population.
Hashimoto's thyroiditis and Graves' disease coexist with celiac disease in about 4-6% of cases versus 1% background. Untreated celiac can also reduce levothyroxine absorption.
Celiac disease prevalence is 5-12% in Down syndrome — roughly 5-10 times background. Screening is part of routine care from age 2-3 onward.
Celiac disease prevalence is 4-8% in Turner syndrome. Screening is recommended at diagnosis and every 2-5 years thereafter.

Selective IgA deficiency occurs in 2-3% of celiac patients, roughly 10-15 times background. Importantly, this affects serologic testing — IgA tTG can be falsely negative, so total IgA should be measured at the same time.
Women are diagnosed 2-3x more often than men. Whether this reflects true biological susceptibility or higher rates of clinical presentation and testing is debated.

riskFactors.factor: Northern European, North African, and Indian Punjabi ancestry
riskFactors.description: Highest prevalence rates are documented in Finland (~2%), Sweden, Ireland, North Africa (Saharawi children up to 5.6%), and Punjabi populations in India and the UK due to high HLA-DQ2 carrier frequencies.

Prospective cohort data (TEDDY study, Lindfors 2020) link recurrent rotavirus infections in genetically at-risk infants to increased risk of celiac autoimmunity. Reovirus has been mechanistically linked to loss of oral tolerance to gluten.

affectedDemographics: Global biopsy-confirmed prevalence ~0.7%; seroprevalence ~1.4% across continents (Singh et al, 2018)
Highest rates in Finland (~2%) and Saharawi children in North Africa (~5.6% — the highest documented globally)
India: Northern Punjabi population carries prevalence around 1%, while southern Indian rates are 5-10x lower
Women diagnosed 2-3 times more often than men, with female-to-male ratio narrowing in older age
Roughly 70% of celiac cases worldwide remain undiagnosed — the diagnostic iceberg
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of celiac disease begins with serology while the patient is still eating a gluten-containing diet — at least 3 g of gluten daily (about two slices of wheat bread) for 6-8 weeks before testing. The first-line test is serum IgA tissue transglutaminase antibody (IgA tTG), which has a pooled sensitivity around 95% and specificity around 95% in symptomatic adults. Total serum IgA is measured at the same time to identify selective IgA deficiency (2-3% of celiac patients), in which case IgG-based testing — IgG deamidated gliadin peptide (DGP) or IgG tTG — is substituted. Endomysial antibody (EMA) is highly specific (close to 100%) and is used as a confirmatory tier when IgA tTG is positive but biopsy is delayed or uncertain. The reference standard in adults is upper endoscopy with duodenal biopsy, taking at least four samples from the distal duodenum plus two from the duodenal bulb (ACG 2023). Histology is graded by the Marsh-Oberhuber system, and a finding of Marsh II or higher in a seropositive patient confirms the diagnosis. ESPGHAN 2020 endorses a no-biopsy pathway in symptomatic children when IgA tTG exceeds 10x the upper limit of normal, confirmed by a positive EMA on a separate sample — this has been adopted into pediatric practice in much of Europe and increasingly in adults under selected criteria. HLA-DQ2/DQ8 genotyping has high negative predictive value: a negative result essentially rules out celiac disease and is useful in equivocal scenarios such as a patient already on a gluten-free diet or biopsy-positive but seronegative cases. Differential diagnosis includes non-celiac gluten sensitivity, wheat allergy, microscopic colitis, tropical sprue, common variable immunodeficiency enteropathy, autoimmune enteropathy, and small intestinal bacterial overgrowth.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line serology with sensitivity ~95% and specificity ~95% in symptomatic adults on a gluten-containing diet. Must be performed alongside total IgA to detect selective IgA deficiency.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; results return in 1-3 days. The test is unreliable on a gluten-free diet — eat gluten daily for at least 6-8 weeks beforehand if you have already cut it out.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects selective IgA deficiency (2-3% of celiac patients) which causes false-negative IgA tTG. If deficient, switch to IgG-based serology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Drawn from the same blood sample as tTG. Same-day result in most labs.

diagnosticTests.test: IgG deamidated gliadin peptide (IgG DGP) and IgG tTG
diagnosticTests.purpose: Substitute serology in IgA-deficient patients, in children under 2 years, and in selected adults with strong clinical suspicion but negative IgA tTG.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single venous blood draw, results in 1-3 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirmatory test with specificity close to 100%. Used to support a no-biopsy pediatric diagnosis when IgA tTG exceeds 10x ULN, or to clarify ambiguous serology.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on a separate blood sample by indirect immunofluorescence; results in 3-7 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reference standard in adults. ACG 2023 recommends at least 4 biopsies from the distal duodenum plus 2 from the duodenal bulb. Histology graded by Marsh-Oberhuber; Marsh II or higher in a seropositive patient confirms diagnosis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under conscious sedation as a day-case procedure lasting 15-20 minutes. A thin flexible scope is passed through the mouth into the duodenum; small tissue samples are taken painlessly. Most patients go home the same day; throat is mildly sore for a few hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Negative predictive value over 99% — absence of both haplotypes essentially excludes celiac disease. Useful when patients are already on a gluten-free diet, when biopsy and serology disagree, or when screening high-risk groups.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Single venous blood or buccal swab sample. Results in 1-2 weeks. Test is unaffected by diet.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and tracks deficiencies at diagnosis: complete blood count, ferritin, vitamin B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium, magnesium, zinc, and liver enzymes. DEXA bone densitometry at or shortly after diagnosis (ACG 2023).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Routine fasting blood draw for the panel; DEXA scan takes 10-15 minutes, painless, very low radiation.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for evaluating complications — non-responsive or refractory celiac disease, ulcerative jejunoileitis, suspected enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma, or small bowel adenocarcinoma.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Capsule endoscopy: swallow a small pill-camera, wear a recorder for 8 hours. MR enterography: drink contrast solution then lie in the scanner for 30-45 minutes.

treatmentOverview: The cornerstone treatment for celiac disease is a strict, lifelong gluten-free diet — complete avoidance of wheat, barley, rye, and contaminated oats, with foods labeled 'gluten-free' permitted to contain under 20 parts per million of gluten under Codex Alimentarius, EU, FDA, and FSSAI standards. There is currently no approved pharmacologic alternative, although several agents are in clinical development. Within 4-8 weeks of dietary adherence, most patients report symptom improvement; serologic normalization (negative IgA tTG) typically takes 6-24 months; histologic recovery to Marsh 0-I occurs in 65-85% of adults at 1-2 years (ACG 2023) and is faster in children. Initial management bundles dietitian-led education (a registered dietitian with celiac expertise is recommended at diagnosis and at follow-up by both ACG 2023 and ESsCD 2019), correction of nutritional deficiencies (iron, B12, folate, vitamin D, calcium, zinc), DEXA scan to baseline bone density, and pneumococcal vaccination because functional hyposplenism is common in adult celiac disease. Annual follow-up tracks symptoms, growth in children, serology, complete blood count, ferritin, vitamin D, liver enzymes, thyroid function, and adherence to the gluten-free diet. Persistent symptoms or persistently positive serology beyond 12 months prompts a structured non-responsive celiac workup: dietitian review for inadvertent gluten exposure (the dominant cause), repeat biopsy, evaluation for microscopic colitis, lactose intolerance, pancreatic insufficiency, SIBO, and refractory celiac disease. Emerging adjunct therapies in clinical trials include latiglutenase (an oral gluten-degrading enzyme), TAK-101 nanoparticle-based tolerance induction, and IMU-856 — none are approved as substitutes for the gluten-free diet at the time of writing. Larazotide acetate, the most advanced phase-3 candidate, did not meet its primary endpoint in 2022.

medicalTreatments.name: Strict lifelong gluten-free diet (<20 ppm gluten)
medicalTreatments.description: Complete avoidance of wheat, barley, rye, malt, brewer's yeast, and contaminated oats. Certified gluten-free oats are tolerated by most patients (about 95%) but a minority react and should avoid them. Cross-contamination from shared toasters, fryers, flour dust, and unlabeled processed foods is the dominant cause of treatment failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom improvement in 70-90% within 4-8 weeks; serologic normalization in 60-80% by 12 months; mucosal healing (Marsh 0-I) in 65-85% of adults at 1-2 years and over 95% of children.

medicalTreatments.name: Iron, vitamin D, calcium, B12, folate, and zinc repletion
medicalTreatments.description: Replace deficiencies identified at diagnosis with targeted oral or, if severe, intravenous supplementation. Oral iron is usually adequate but absorption is poor until villi recover, so retest at 3-6 months. Vitamin D and calcium are crucial for bone recovery alongside DEXA monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Iron stores normalize in 60-70% within 6-12 months on dietary adherence plus oral iron; bone mineral density rises 5% in the first year and continues for 2-3 years.

medicalTreatments.name: Pneumococcal and influenza vaccination
medicalTreatments.description: Functional hyposplenism is present in roughly one third of adults with celiac disease and confers increased risk of encapsulated organism infection. Both ACG 2023 and ESsCD 2019 recommend pneumococcal (PCV13/PCV15 followed by PPSV23) and annual influenza vaccination at or shortly after diagnosis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Substantially reduces pneumococcal and influenza infection rates in this population; specific efficacy mirrors that in other functional hyposplenism cohorts.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine for coexisting autoimmune thyroiditis
medicalTreatments.description: Around 4-6% of adults with celiac disease have hypothyroidism from Hashimoto's thyroiditis. Once celiac disease is treated and absorption restored, levothyroxine doses often need to be reduced; thyroid function should be rechecked 6-8 weeks after starting the gluten-free diet.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Standard hypothyroidism response rates apply (>90% biochemical control on appropriate dose).

medicalTreatments.name: Open-capsule budesonide (9 mg daily) for refractory celiac disease
medicalTreatments.description: Topically active steroid used in refractory celiac disease type I and as bridge therapy in severe symptoms despite a strict gluten-free diet. Open-capsule administration releases drug throughout the small bowel rather than just the terminal ileum.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in 70-90% of refractory type I patients in case series; histologic improvement variable. Reserved for specialist use after exclusion of ongoing gluten exposure.

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone for dermatitis herpetiformis
medicalTreatments.description: Used short-term to control intense itch and rash while the gluten-free diet takes effect. Typically started at 25-50 mg daily and tapered as the diet controls disease. Requires G6PD screening before initiation due to risk of hemolysis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Itching resolves in 24-72 hours in over 90% of patients; rash settles within 1-2 weeks. Long-term control of dermatitis herpetiformis depends on strict adherence to the gluten-free diet.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical resection of complicated small bowel adenocarcinoma or enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for malignant complications of long-standing or refractory celiac disease — small bowel adenocarcinoma and enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL). Combined surgical resection, chemotherapy, and in some EATL cases autologous stem cell transplant are used.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Five-year survival in EATL remains 8-20% despite aggressive therapy; small bowel adenocarcinoma 5-year survival 30-50% when resected early.

alternativeTreatments.name: Certified gluten-free oats (in tolerant patients)
alternativeTreatments.description: About 95% of celiac patients tolerate pure, uncontaminated oats. Adds soluble fiber, B vitamins, and palatability. Start cautiously and monitor symptoms and serology — a minority of patients react to avenin and must avoid all oats.

alternativeTreatments.name: Probiotics targeting the celiac microbiome
alternativeTreatments.description: Selected strains (Bifidobacterium longum, Lactobacillus rhamnosus) show modest symptomatic benefit in small randomized trials in patients on a gluten-free diet with persistent bloating. Quality of evidence is low; cannot substitute for diet.

whySeeSpecialist: Refer to a gastroenterologist for confirmatory duodenal biopsy in any adult with positive celiac serology, for evaluation of persistent symptoms or positive serology after 12 months on a strict gluten-free diet, for suspected refractory celiac disease, and for surveillance of complications including small bowel lymphoma or adenocarcinoma. A registered dietitian with celiac expertise is essential at diagnosis and at follow-up: structured dietitian-led education is associated with measurably better adherence and faster mucosal healing.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a gastroenterologist with experience in autoimmune enteropathies and access to a dietitian trained in gluten-free diet counseling. Confirm the practice routinely takes 4-6 duodenal biopsies (distal duodenum plus bulb) — single-site biopsies miss patchy villous atrophy in up to 10% of cases. Ask whether the unit performs HLA-DQ2/DQ8 testing in-house and runs a structured non-responsive celiac protocol. Continuity of care matters — celiac disease is a lifetime condition and a long-term relationship with a clinician familiar with your history improves outcomes.
preventionStrategies: There is no proven way to prevent celiac disease — neither delaying nor accelerating gluten introduction in infancy reduces incidence (PreventCD and CELIPREV trials)
Breastfeeding does not prevent celiac disease but may delay onset in genetically susceptible infants

Screen first-degree relatives of patients with confirmed celiac disease at least once after age 3-4, even when asymptomatic, because over 50% of affected relatives have non-classical or silent presentations
Screen patients with type 1 diabetes, autoimmune thyroid disease, Down syndrome, Turner syndrome, and unexplained iron deficiency anemia, osteoporosis, or transaminitis
Once diagnosed, strict adherence to a gluten-free diet prevents long-term complications including osteoporosis, infertility, refractory disease, and enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma
lifestyleModifications: Read every ingredient label every time — manufacturers reformulate, and a previously safe product can become unsafe overnight
Designate gluten-free zones at home: a separate toaster, cutting board, colander, and butter dish prevent cross-contamination in mixed households
When eating out, call ahead to ask whether the kitchen has a dedicated gluten-free preparation area, and avoid shared fryers where gluten-containing foods are cooked
Carry a small written restaurant card in the local language when traveling; international celiac associations publish translated versions for over 60 countries
Join a national celiac society — Coeliac UK, Celiac Disease Foundation (US), Beyond Celiac, AOECS (Europe) — for regularly updated safe-food lists and recipes
Track symptoms and inadvertent exposures in a simple diary; patterns often identify hidden sources of gluten such as soy sauce, malt vinegar, or shared kitchen utensils
Inform anesthesia, dental, and pharmacy teams before procedures — some medications, supplements, and barium contrasts contain gluten as an excipient

recommended: Naturally gluten-free grains and starches: rice, corn, quinoa, millet, buckwheat, amaranth, sorghum, teff, cassava
Fresh meat, fish, eggs, poultry, and unprocessed dairy
All fresh fruits, vegetables, legumes, nuts, and seeds in their natural form
Certified gluten-free oats for tolerant patients (about 95% of celiac patients tolerate them)
Foods explicitly labeled 'gluten-free' under FDA (<20 ppm), EU, FSSAI, or Codex standards
Naturally gluten-free flours for baking: rice flour, almond flour, coconut flour, chickpea (besan) flour
avoid: Wheat in all forms: bread, pasta, couscous, semolina, durum, spelt, kamut, einkorn, farro, triticale
Barley (including malt, malt vinegar, malt flavoring, and most beers) and rye
Standard oats unless certified gluten-free, due to cross-contamination during farming and milling
Soy sauce containing wheat (use tamari), and most commercial gravies, soups, and seasoning blends that use wheat-based thickeners
Communion wafers, many medications and supplements with wheat-derived excipients (ask the pharmacist), and some lipsticks and toothpastes
Shared kitchen items contaminated with wheat flour or crumbs — toasters, colanders, wooden boards, and deep fryers

exerciseGuidelines: Regular weight-bearing and resistance exercise is strongly recommended to rebuild bone mass once the gluten-free diet has begun, given the high prevalence of osteopenia at diagnosis. Aim for 150 minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week plus 2-3 resistance sessions. Endurance athletes should plan gluten-free fueling carefully: many sports drinks, gels, and recovery products contain gluten-derived ingredients. Performance typically returns to baseline once nutritional deficiencies and anemia are corrected, usually within 6-12 months.
dailyManagement: Read every food label every time, even on products you have bought before — recipe changes can introduce gluten
Keep separate kitchen tools (toaster, butter, jam, cutting boards) in shared households to prevent cross-contamination
Take prescribed nutritional supplements (iron, vitamin D, calcium, B12) with meals as directed and recheck levels at 3-6 months
Plan ahead for travel and eating out: research restaurants, pack gluten-free snacks, and carry an allergy card
Use a celiac-society-vetted app or website to check ingredients and packaged foods quickly
Watch for symptoms after new processed foods or restaurant meals — early recognition of accidental exposure helps refine your safe-food list
prognosis: With strict, lifelong adherence to a gluten-free diet, the long-term prognosis for celiac disease is excellent and life expectancy approaches that of the general population. Symptoms improve within weeks, serology normalizes by 6-24 months, and mucosal healing reaches Marsh 0-I in 65-85% of adults and over 95% of children within 1-2 years. Bone mineral density rises measurably in the first year and continues to recover for several years. Untreated or partially treated disease carries a meaningfully increased risk of osteoporosis, infertility, anemia, autoimmune comorbidity, refractory celiac disease (1-2% of adults), small bowel adenocarcinoma, and enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma — the last of these accounts for most of the modestly elevated mortality reported in older cohort studies. The strongest predictor of good outcome is dietary adherence, which is best supported by ongoing dietitian review, family education, and engagement with celiac patient organizations.
recoveryTimeline: Symptom improvement begins within 1-4 weeks of a strict gluten-free diet for most adults. Energy, mood, and bowel habits typically stabilize by 2-3 months. Serology (IgA tTG) trends downward by 6 months and normalizes in 60-80% of patients by 12 months. Histologic recovery to Marsh 0-I takes 1-2 years in 65-85% of adults; children heal faster, usually within 6-12 months. Bone density improvements are measurable on DEXA at 12 months and continue for 2-3 years.
complications: Iron deficiency anemia — present at diagnosis in up to 40% and typically reversible on a gluten-free diet with iron repletion
Osteopenia and osteoporosis — DEXA at diagnosis is standard; bone density improves over 1-3 years on dietary adherence
Refractory celiac disease (1-2% of adults) — persistent or recurrent villous atrophy despite 12+ months of strict diet, with Type II carrying high risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma
Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL) — rare but accounts for most of the excess mortality in celiac disease; 5-year survival 8-20%
Small bowel adenocarcinoma — relative risk increased roughly 10-fold over background, although absolute incidence remains low
Infertility, recurrent miscarriage, and earlier menopause — reproductive outcomes improve substantially on a strict gluten-free diet
Functional hyposplenism with increased pneumococcal infection risk — pneumococcal vaccination is recommended at diagnosis
Neurologic complications including peripheral neuropathy and gluten ataxia — typically stabilize or partially reverse on the gluten-free diet

supportResources.name: Celiac Disease Foundation (US)
supportResources.description: Patient education, ingredient lookup, advocacy, and physician finder for the United States.

supportResources.name: Beyond Celiac
supportResources.description: US patient advocacy group focused on celiac research, the Go Beyond Celiac patient registry, and lived-experience resources.

supportResources.name: Coeliac UK
supportResources.description: UK patient organization with comprehensive food directory, restaurant guide, and clinical resources.

supportResources.name: Association of European Coeliac Societies (AOECS)
supportResources.description: Federation of European celiac societies; maintains the Crossed Grain trademark licensing scheme for certified gluten-free products.

confusedWithConditions.name: Non-Celiac Gluten Sensitivity
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Non-celiac gluten sensitivity produces similar gut and extra-intestinal symptoms after gluten ingestion but lacks the celiac-specific serology (negative IgA tTG and EMA) and normal duodenal biopsy on a gluten-containing diet. There is no autoimmune attack on the small bowel and no risk of EATL or villous atrophy. Diagnosis is by exclusion after both celiac disease and wheat allergy have been ruled out.

confusedWithConditions.name: Wheat Allergy (IgE-mediated food allergy)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Wheat allergy is an immediate IgE-mediated reaction to wheat proteins producing hives, angioedema, wheezing, or anaphylaxis within minutes to hours. Skin-prick or specific IgE testing is positive; tTG is negative; the small bowel is histologically normal. Patients can sometimes tolerate barley and rye, unlike in celiac disease.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable Bowel Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: IBS shares bloating, abdominal pain, and altered bowel habit with celiac disease but produces no villous atrophy, no positive celiac serology, and no nutritional deficiencies. ACG 2023 recommends serologic screening for celiac disease in any patient diagnosed with IBS-D or IBS-mixed, given a higher background prevalence.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lactose Intolerance
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lactose intolerance is a brush-border lactase deficiency producing diarrhea, bloating, and gas after dairy. It frequently coexists with untreated celiac disease as a secondary phenomenon from villous damage and resolves when villi heal. Hydrogen breath test is positive; celiac serology and biopsy are negative.

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's Disease
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease is a transmural inflammatory bowel disease most often affecting the terminal ileum and colon, with discontinuous lesions, granulomas, fistulae, and strictures. Serology and biopsy patterns are distinct from celiac disease, although both can produce malabsorption, weight loss, and anemia.

relatedConditions.name: Dermatitis Herpetiformis
relatedConditions.relevance: The cutaneous manifestation of celiac disease; present in 10-15% of patients and responds to the same gluten-free diet.

relatedConditions.name: Type 1 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares HLA-DQ2/DQ8 susceptibility; 5-10% of type 1 diabetes patients have biopsy-confirmed celiac disease.

relatedConditions.name: Hashimoto's Disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Autoimmune thyroid disease coexists with celiac disease in 4-6% of cases; untreated celiac can reduce levothyroxine absorption.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Calcium and vitamin D malabsorption produce osteopenia in 30-40% of newly diagnosed adults; DEXA at diagnosis is standard practice.

relatedConditions.name: Iron Deficiency Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Often the only sign of adult celiac disease — ACG 2023 recommends celiac serology in any adult with iron deficiency anemia that fails to respond to oral iron.

relatedConditions.name: Autoimmune Thyroiditis
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares HLA susceptibility; periodic thyroid function testing is part of long-term celiac follow-up.

faqs.question: Is celiac disease curable?
faqs.answer: Celiac disease is not curable but is fully controllable with a strict lifelong gluten-free diet. The small intestine heals in 65-85% of adults within 1-2 years, symptoms resolve, and long-term complications are largely prevented.

faqs.question: What is the gold-standard test for celiac disease?
faqs.answer: In adults the gold standard is duodenal biopsy on upper endoscopy, with at least four samples from the distal duodenum plus two from the bulb. Marsh II or higher in a patient with positive IgA tTG confirms celiac disease.

faqs.question: Can celiac disease be diagnosed without a biopsy?
faqs.answer: In symptomatic children, ESPGHAN 2020 allows no-biopsy diagnosis when IgA tTG exceeds 10x the upper limit of normal and a separate sample is endomysial antibody positive. In adults, biopsy remains standard outside selected high-titer cases.

faqs.question: Do I need to keep eating gluten before testing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Serology and biopsy are unreliable on a gluten-free diet. Eat at least 3 grams of gluten daily — roughly two slices of wheat bread — for 6-8 weeks before testing.

faqs.question: What are the most common symptoms in adults?
faqs.answer: Most adults present with non-classical features: iron deficiency anemia, fatigue, bloating, osteopenia, transaminitis, or infertility. Classical diarrhea and weight loss now account for under half of new adult diagnoses.

faqs.question: Is there a medication for celiac disease?
faqs.answer: No approved medication replaces the gluten-free diet. Agents in clinical trials include latiglutenase, TAK-101, and IMU-856; larazotide acetate failed its phase-3 endpoint in 2022. Dapsone is used short-term for dermatitis herpetiformis.

faqs.question: How strict does a gluten-free diet need to be?
faqs.answer: Strictly under 20 parts per million — the international threshold for gluten-free labeling. Repeated small exposures (crumbs, shared toasters, contaminated fryers) prevent mucosal healing and account for most cases of persistent symptoms.

faqs.question: Can I eat oats with celiac disease?
faqs.answer: About 95% of patients tolerate certified gluten-free oats. Standard oats are usually contaminated with wheat during farming and milling and must be avoided. A small minority react to avenin itself and should avoid all oats.

faqs.question: How long does it take to heal on a gluten-free diet?
faqs.answer: Symptoms typically improve within 1-4 weeks. IgA tTG normalizes in 60-80% by 12 months, and mucosal healing reaches Marsh 0-I in 65-85% of adults at 1-2 years. Children heal faster, usually within 6-12 months.

faqs.question: What is dermatitis herpetiformis?
faqs.answer: Dermatitis herpetiformis is the skin form of celiac disease — an intensely itchy, symmetric vesicular rash on elbows, knees, buttocks, and scalp. It affects 10-15% of celiac patients and clears on a strict gluten-free diet.

faqs.question: Should my family be screened for celiac disease?
faqs.answer: Yes. First-degree relatives carry roughly a 10% lifetime risk. ACG 2023 recommends screening symptomatic first-degree relatives with IgA tTG and total IgA. One-time HLA-DQ2/DQ8 testing can also rule it out for life.

faqs.question: What is the difference between celiac disease and gluten sensitivity?
faqs.answer: Celiac disease is autoimmune with positive serology, villous atrophy, and risk of long-term complications. Non-celiac gluten sensitivity has negative celiac serology, normal biopsy, and no autoimmune damage; it is diagnosed by exclusion.

faqs.question: Can celiac disease cause infertility?
faqs.answer: Yes. Untreated celiac disease is linked to delayed menarche, infertility, recurrent miscarriage, and earlier menopause at 2-4 times background rates. Reproductive outcomes improve on a strict gluten-free diet.

faqs.question: Does celiac disease increase the risk of cancer?
faqs.answer: Long-standing or untreated celiac disease modestly raises the risk of enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma (EATL) and small bowel adenocarcinoma, though absolute risk is low. Strict gluten-free diet adherence substantially reduces these risks.

faqs.question: Can children outgrow celiac disease?
faqs.answer: No. Celiac disease is lifelong. Some children appear to tolerate gluten reintroduction without symptoms, but silent mucosal damage continues. Current pediatric guidelines do not support reintroducing gluten once the diagnosis is confirmed.

faqs.question: Why is iron deficiency anemia linked to celiac disease?
faqs.answer: Iron is absorbed in the duodenum, exactly where celiac disease causes the most villous damage. Iron deficiency anemia unresponsive to oral iron is one of the strongest indications for celiac screening and is found in up to 40% of newly diagnosed adults.

faqs.question: What is refractory celiac disease?
faqs.answer: Refractory celiac disease is persistent symptoms and villous atrophy despite a strict gluten-free diet for 12+ months, after excluding hidden gluten exposure and other diagnoses. It affects 1-2% of adults. Type II carries a high risk of EATL.

faqs.question: Do I need a DEXA scan after diagnosis?
faqs.answer: Yes. ACG 2023 and ESsCD 2019 recommend baseline DEXA bone densitometry at or shortly after diagnosis because 30-40% of newly diagnosed adults have osteopenia or osteoporosis. Repeat DEXA at 1-2 years tracks recovery and identifies anyone who needs further bone-targeted treatment.

faqs.question: Can celiac disease affect the brain?
faqs.answer: Yes. Neurologic features include persistent fatigue and brain fog, peripheral neuropathy, and gluten ataxia — a cerebellar syndrome with imbalance and unsteady gait. Most neurologic symptoms stabilize or partially reverse on a strict gluten-free diet, though improvement is slower than gut symptoms.

faqs.question: How much does celiac disease testing and treatment cost?
faqs.answer: Serologic panels and endoscopy with biopsy are usually covered by insurers and national health systems; out-of-pocket panel costs typically run USD 50-200. The bigger ongoing cost is food: certified gluten-free staples cost 2-3 times their wheat equivalents.

faqs.question: Can I drink alcohol with celiac disease?
faqs.answer: Most distilled spirits, wines, and ciders are gluten-free because distillation removes gluten proteins. Standard beers brewed from barley are not safe; gluten-free beers (sorghum, millet, rice) and certified gluten-removed beers are alternatives.

faqs.question: When should I see a gastroenterologist about celiac disease?
faqs.answer: See a gastroenterologist for biopsy confirmation after positive serology, for persistent symptoms or positive serology after 12 months on a strict diet, for suspected refractory disease, or for red-flag features such as bleeding or unexplained weight loss.

metaTitle: Celiac Disease: Symptoms, Causes & Treatment | aihealz
metaDescription: Celiac disease: autoimmune reaction to gluten damaging the small intestine. Symptoms, diagnosis, gluten-free diet, and prognosis reviewed by our doctors.
